items.prompt_md: In the longest common subsequence table, what does dp[i][j] represent?
options: The length of the LCS between the first i characters of A and the first j characters of B
Whether A[i] equals B[j]
The length of the longest common substring ending exactly at A[i] and B[j]
The total number of common characters anywhere in A and B
items.answer: The length of the LCS between the first i characters of A and the first j characters of B
items.explanation_md: dp[i][j] is scoped to prefixes A[:i] and B[:j], which is why row 0 and column 0 (empty prefixes) are seeded with zeros.

items.prompt_md: When A[i-1] == B[j-1] in the LCS recurrence, what does dp[i][j] become?
options: max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
items.explanation_md: A matching pair of characters can always be appended to the LCS of the prefixes before them, extending it by exactly one.

items.prompt_md: When A[i-1] != B[j-1], what does dp[i][j] become?
options: max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
items.answer: max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
items.explanation_md: If the last characters don't match, the LCS can't include both, so the best is whichever of dropping A's last char or B's last char gives the longer subsequence.

items.prompt_md: Why does the LCS table use size (m+1) x (n+1) instead of m x n?
options: To reserve a row and column of zeros representing the empty-prefix base case
Because the recurrence needs one extra cell for rounding
To leave room for the final answer to be stored separately
Because string indices in this language start at 1
items.answer: To reserve a row and column of zeros representing the empty-prefix base case
items.explanation_md: dp[0][j] and dp[i][0] represent comparing against an empty string, which always has LCS length 0 — the base case the recurrence builds on.

items.prompt_md: In edit distance, what does dp[i][j] represent?
options: The minimum number of insert/delete/replace operations to turn A[:i] into B[:j]
Whether A[:i] and B[:j] are already equal
The length of the longest common subsequence of A[:i] and B[:j]
The number of characters that differ between A[:i] and B[:j]
items.answer: The minimum number of insert/delete/replace operations to turn A[:i] into B[:j]
items.explanation_md: Edit distance is a minimization over edit operations on prefixes, unlike LCS which maximizes a matching count.

items.prompt_md: In edit distance, when A[i-1] == B[j-1], what is dp[i][j]?
options: dp[i-1][j-1] — no operation needed, so it inherits the cost of the smaller prefixes
dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 — a match still costs one operation
0, since a match always resets the cost
items.answer: dp[i-1][j-1] — no operation needed, so it inherits the cost of the smaller prefixes
items.explanation_md: If the current characters already match, no edit is spent aligning them, so the cost is exactly whatever it took to align the prefixes before them.

items.prompt_md: In edit distance, when A[i-1] != B[j-1], what is dp[i][j]?
options: 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) — the cheapest of delete, insert, or replace
max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 always, ignoring the other two neighbors
items.answer: 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) — the cheapest of delete, insert, or replace
items.explanation_md: A mismatch forces one operation; the recurrence picks whichever of delete (dp[i-1][j]), insert (dp[i][j-1]), or replace (dp[i-1][j-1]) leaves the cheapest remaining subproblem.

items.prompt_md: What are dp[i][0] and dp[0][j] seeded to in the edit distance table, and why?
options: dp[i][0] = i and dp[0][j] = j, the cost of inserting or deleting every remaining character
dp[i][0] = 0 and dp[0][j] = 0, since empty strings always match for free
dp[i][0] = infinity and dp[0][j] = infinity, since one side is empty
dp[i][0] = dp[0][j] = 1 for every i and j
items.answer: dp[i][0] = i and dp[0][j] = j, the cost of inserting or deleting every remaining character
items.explanation_md: Turning a length-i prefix into the empty string takes i deletions, and turning the empty string into a length-j prefix takes j insertions — the base cases the recurrence builds on.

items.prompt_md: Why must the two-sequence DP table be filled with rows increasing (i from 1 to m) and, within each row, columns increasing (j from 1 to n)?
options: Because every cell's recurrence reads dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1], all of which must already be computed
Because strings must be read left to right in this language
Because it produces a smaller table than any other fill order
Because the answer is only correct if computed in alphabetical order of characters
items.answer: Because every cell's recurrence reads dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1], all of which must already be computed
items.explanation_md: Row-major, left-to-right fill guarantees all three predecessor cells a given cell depends on are already finished.

items.prompt_md: A common bug in LCS implementations is off-by-one indexing between the dp table and the strings. What is the correct character comparison for dp[i][j]?
options: Compare A[i-1] and B[j-1], since dp[i][j] represents prefixes of length i and j (0-indexed strings)
Compare A[i] and B[j] directly
Compare A[i-1] and B[j], mixing 0- and 1-indexing intentionally
Compare A[i] and B[j-1]
items.answer: Compare A[i-1] and B[j-1], since dp[i][j] represents prefixes of length i and j (0-indexed strings)
items.explanation_md: Because dp is offset by one to make room for the empty-prefix row/column, the character at prefix-position i is A[i-1] in the 0-indexed string.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of filling the LCS table for strings of length m and n?
options: O(m * n)
O(m + n)
O(m * n * log(m+n))
items.answer: O(m * n)
items.explanation_md: Every one of the (m+1)*(n+1) cells is computed once in constant time from its three neighbors, giving O(m*n) time.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the full 2-D LCS table before any rolling-array optimization?
options: O(m * n)
O(m + n)
O(min(m, n))
items.answer: O(m * n)
items.explanation_md: The straightforward implementation allocates one entry per (i, j) pair, so it uses O(m*n) space.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the brute-force approach that enumerates all subsequences of A to check against B?
options: Exponential, roughly O(2^m * n)
O(m * n)
O(m log n)
O(m + n)
items.answer: Exponential, roughly O(2^m * n)
items.explanation_md: There are 2^m subsequences of A, and checking each against B costs up to O(n), so brute force is exponential — exactly what the DP table avoids by sharing overlapping prefix subproblems.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the edit distance DP table for strings of length m and n?
options: O(m * n)
O(m + n)
items.answer: O(m * n)
items.explanation_md: Each of the (m+1)*(n+1) cells does a constant amount of work (comparing three neighbors), so the total is O(m*n) — the min-of-three check is a constant factor, not an asymptotic one.

items.prompt_md: If you only need the length of the LCS (not the subsequence itself), what space complexity can you achieve by rolling the table down to two rows?
options: O(min(m, n))
O(m * n)
O(m + n) always, regardless of which axis you roll
items.answer: O(min(m, n))
items.explanation_md: Since each row only depends on the row above it, you can keep just two rows at a time; orienting the shorter string along the rolled axis gets you down to O(min(m, n)).

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and asked for the length of their longest common subsequence, where characters may be skipped in either string but relative order must be preserved. Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table over the two string indices, branching on whether the current characters match
A single 1-D DP array over one string's positions
Sort both strings and count matching characters
Two pointers walking both strings from opposite ends
items.answer: 2-D DP table over the two string indices, branching on whether the current characters match
items.explanation_md: Two sequences, each contributing its own changing dimension, with a match/no-match branch — that's the fingerprint of two-sequence DP over a 2-D table.

items.prompt_md: You need the minimum number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions to transform one string into another. Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table indexed by (position in A, position in B), taking the min of the three edit operations
Greedy: substitute mismatched characters one at a time, left to right
Backtracking over every possible sequence of edits
A 1-D DP array over the length difference between the two strings
items.answer: 2-D DP table indexed by (position in A, position in B), taking the min of the three edit operations
items.explanation_md: Two independently changing sequence positions with optimal-substructure edit costs is exactly the two-sequence DP trigger; a left-to-right greedy substitution can miss a cheaper insert/delete combination.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and asked whether one is a subsequence of the other (not the longest common one — just yes/no, and every character of the shorter string must appear in order in the longer one). Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers, advancing through the longer string and only advancing the shorter string's pointer on a match
A full 2-D DP table computing LCS length and comparing it to the shorter string's length
Backtracking over every possible subsequence of the longer string
A 1-D DP array over just the longer string
items.answer: Two pointers, advancing through the longer string and only advancing the shorter string's pointer on a match
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: a plain yes/no subsequence check needs no optimization over alternatives, so a single greedy two-pointer scan settles it in linear time — the full 2-D LCS table is unnecessary machinery for a question that isn't asking for the longest anything.

items.prompt_md: You're given a single string and asked for the length of its longest palindromic subsequence (comparing the string against its own reverse). Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table over two indices into the same string, same recurrence shape as LCS
A 1-D DP array over just one index
Two pointers converging from both ends of the string
Backtracking that tries every subsequence
items.answer: 2-D DP table over two indices into the same string, same recurrence shape as LCS
items.explanation_md: Comparing a string against its own reverse is exactly an LCS between two sequences (the string and its reverse), so it inherits the same 2-D, match/no-match table structure.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and asked to count the number of distinct ways one string can be formed as a subsequence of the other (a counting question, not a longest-length question). Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table over the two string indices, summing ways rather than taking a max
Backtracking that enumerates every subsequence and checks equality
A greedy match of characters left to right, counting each match once
A 1-D DP array indexed only by the shorter string's length
items.answer: 2-D DP table over the two string indices, summing ways rather than taking a max
items.explanation_md: Counting distinct subsequence-formations still depends on both string positions simultaneously, so it keeps the two-sequence 2-D table shape — only the combination rule (sum instead of max) changes.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings of equal length and asked for the minimum number of positions where you'd need to substitute a character in A to make it exactly equal to B (no insertions or deletions allowed, only substitutions at matching positions). Which approach fits?
options: A single linear pass counting positions where A[k] != B[k]
A full 2-D edit-distance DP table allowing insert, delete, and substitute
2-D LCS DP table, then subtract the LCS length from the string length
Backtracking over every possible substitution pattern
items.answer: A single linear pass counting positions where A[k] != B[k]
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: with equal-length strings and only substitutions allowed at aligned positions, there's no alignment choice left to optimize over — a single pass comparing corresponding characters answers it directly, without any DP table.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and asked for the length of their longest common substring (must be contiguous in both, not just in order). Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table where dp[i][j] is the length of a common substring ending exactly at A[i-1] and B[j-1], reset to 0 on a mismatch
The same LCS recurrence unchanged, since substring and subsequence use identical tables
A 1-D DP array over the length difference between the two strings
Two pointers scanning both strings simultaneously
items.answer: 2-D DP table where dp[i][j] is the length of a common substring ending exactly at A[i-1] and B[j-1], reset to 0 on a mismatch
items.explanation_md: Still a two-sequence 2-D table, but the recurrence must reset to 0 on a mismatch (rather than falling back to a max of neighbors) because a substring can't skip characters — contiguity changes the transition, not the table shape.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and asked whether they are anagrams of each other (same multiset of characters, order irrelevant). Which approach fits?
options: Count character frequencies in both strings and compare the counts
2-D DP table comparing the two strings position by position
Two pointers walking both strings from opposite ends
Backtracking over every permutation of one string
items.answer: Count character frequencies in both strings and compare the counts
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: order doesn't matter for an anagram check, so there's no alignment or ordering relationship to optimize over — a frequency-count comparison answers it in linear time, no 2-D table needed.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and must find the shortest common supersequence — the shortest string that contains both A and B as subsequences. Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table over the two string indices, building on the LCS recurrence to decide what to merge in
A 1-D DP array over the combined length of A and B
Greedy: interleave characters of A and B in the order they appear
Backtracking over every possible merge of A and B
items.answer: 2-D DP table over the two string indices, building on the LCS recurrence to decide what to merge in
items.explanation_md: The shortest supersequence length is the sum of the two lengths minus their LCS length, so it reuses the same two-index dependency table, just with a different final formula applied to the same cells.

items.prompt_md: You're given a single array of numbers and asked for the length of its longest strictly increasing subsequence (only one array is involved, not a comparison between two). Which approach fits?
options: A 1-D DP array indexed by position, tracking the best subsequence length ending at each index
A 2-D DP table over two indices into the same array, matching pairs like LCS
Two pointers scanning from both ends of the array
Backtracking that tries every subsequence
items.answer: A 1-D DP array indexed by position, tracking the best subsequence length ending at each index
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: with only one sequence in play (not a comparison between two), there's only one changing dimension, so a 1-D DP array — the pattern from the earlier unit — is the right fit, not a 2-D two-sequence table.
